Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine that I stuck to. I'd usually just study whenever I felt like it or when something was due soon. Being a kinesthetic learner, I need to be doing something with my hands or moving around, so sitting at a computer for hours just doesn't work for me. I'd spread out my notes on my bed or floor and try to work through things physically, but it was pretty random.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and cram everything in. With 17 online courses to juggle, I couldn't keep a consistent schedule even if I wanted to. Whatever felt most urgent got my attention first, and the rest just kind of fell through the cracks.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an assignment deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder on my phone and think 'oh right, that exists.' I wasn't logging in out of curiosity or genuine interest most of the time—it was more like obligation mixed with anxiety. When I knew something was due, that's when I'd finally open it up.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, but then life would get in the way and I'd drift off again. Without reliable internet access at home, getting on the LMS was already a hassle, so it never felt natural or automatic to use it regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could watch them, because at least I could pause and rewind when I got lost. I watched quite a bit of video content because it felt more engaging than just reading. The assignment submission portal was essential obviously, and I did pretty well on my actual assignment scores, so that part worked for me at least.
Q6: The discussion forums were basically useless to me—I only posted once the whole semester. I didn't see the point in typing out my thoughts when I could barely keep up with the actual coursework. Reading materials and extra resources got ignored a lot too because without internet at home, I couldn't easily access them when I actually had time to study.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly counted toward my grade or if I could see immediate results from doing it. Like, assignments were worth it because I could see my score afterward and know I accomplished something. Anything that felt vague or optional just got pushed aside because I had too much else going on.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety already through the roof about required stuff, adding optional things on top felt overwhelming. Required activities at least had clear stakes—I knew what would happen if I didn't do them. Optional ones just floated there making me feel guilty for not doing them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs when I could get online, then study them later at home where I don't have internet. I'd write out notes by hand, make flashcards, sometimes even act out concepts or walk around while reciting things because that's how I learn best. A lot of my studying happened with physical materials, not on a screen.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline with downloaded materials or handwritten notes. I'd spend hours going through things at home, but the LMS would never know because I wasn't connected. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other, and that helped way more than anything online. The system only saw me when I submitted stuff or watched videos on campus.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything I could, then not come back for days while I worked through it all offline. It was more efficient for me that way given my internet situation. The LMS probably thought I disappeared, but I was actually studying—just not in a way it could track.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my anxiety spiked about exams. I'd feel paralyzed and avoid logging in because seeing all the work I was behind on made me feel worse. Sometimes I just couldn't face it, so I'd do other things instead. The anxiety was weird because I'm generally resilient, but something about this course structure really got to me.
Q13: Absolutely. With 17 courses and limited resources, I was constantly making choices about what to prioritize. Fatigue was real—I'd be too tired to focus on screens after dealing with everything else in life. Not having internet at home meant I had to plan when and where I could even access the LMS, which added another layer of difficulty.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other assignments or just zoning out. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My body was there, technically engaging, but my brain had checked out. Especially late at night when I was exhausted.
Q15: I avoided exam prep a lot, which is probably why my exam score was so bad. The thought of studying for tests made my anxiety spike, so I'd do assignments instead because at least those felt manageable. I knew exams were important but I kept telling myself I'd get to it later, and then later never really came until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and work. Without that pressure, I'd drift. It wasn't healthy—I'd cram everything last minute and stress myself out—but it was the only system that worked for me given everything else going on.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it actually made me feel better about the course for a bit. Like, seeing that 92 on an assignment reminded me I wasn't completely hopeless. But feedback on exams was discouraging because I kept doing poorly no matter what. Eventually I started avoiding looking at exam feedback because it just made me anxious.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with so much content spread across the LMS. I never felt like I knew where to start or what was most important. Having everything online made it easy to ignore when I wasn't physically in a classroom being held accountable. A more guided, step-by-step structure might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The lack of clear priorities made engagement harder. Everything seemed equally important, so nothing felt urgent until deadlines hit. Also, the heavy reliance on online access was tough for someone like me without reliable internet. What would have helped is more hands-on activities or in-person components since I learn better that way.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to figure out what they need, or just feeling lost and clicking around without direction. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Someone could spend hours on the LMS feeling anxious and confused, opening things but not actually completing work because they don't know where to start.
Q21: That kind of describes me with assignments, actually. My assignment scores were really good even though I wasn't super active on the LMS. I did a lot of work offline, understood the material in my own way, and submitted quality work. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying that happens away from the computer.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental energy spent worrying about the course, the learning that happens through conversation or hands-on practice. They're like looking at footprints without seeing the whole journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, real engagement happens when I'm physically involved—writing, moving, doing something active with the content. It's not about how many times I log in; it's about whether the material actually sticks in my brain.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I dealt with around this course, or how my lack of internet access shaped everything I did. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline with downloaded materials, or how I actually understood concepts well enough to score high on assignments. It also wouldn't show that I'm happy despite struggling—I've got resilience even when things don't go well academically.
